meta.purpose: Drive the termmux / pr-split redesign to deliver a flawless, exemplary implementation of osm pr-split: fully interactive Claude + verification + shell panes with persistent session model, cross-platform support (macOS, Linux, Windows), and exhaustive test coverage. All tasks verified through Rule of Two before marking Done.
meta.architectureReference: scratch/termmux-architecture.md is the authoritative architectural reference. All SessionManager tasks derive from this document. Read it BEFORE executing any task in the 30-39 range.
meta.maintenanceInstruction: KEEP THE PLAN UP TO DATE AS YOU EVOLVE THE CODE. DEVIATIONS TO THE PLAN MUST BE LOGGED WITHIN THE PLAN. THE PLAN MUST BE REASSESSED HOLISTICALLY AFTER ANY CHANGE OF ANY SIZE. This refined blueprint addresses quality issues identified in the Rule of Two analysis.
meta.completionPolicy: YOU WILL COMPLETE 100% OF THE BLUEPRINT. NO PRIORITIES. NO ESTIMATES. This is flat, sequential, and EXHAUSTIVE. All tasks are mandatory. Refine and expand indefinitely after reaching stable state.

globalAlerts.warning: Use GNU Make and project custom targets in config.mk. Prefer make make-all-with-log, make test-prsplit-fast, make test-prsplit-all, make integration-test-prsplit-mcp, make integration-test-termmux, make make-all-in-container, make make-all-run-windows for verification. All checks must pass on all platforms before marking tasks Done.

Acceptance criteria must describe USER VALUE and end-to-end functionality, not just architectural changes. Every task must answer: 'What can the user or system now do that it couldn't before?' A task is only Done when acceptance is fully satisfied and verified through Rule of Two.
JS and Go API parity is mandatory. Do not leave JavaScript call sites pretending termmux supports APIs that the Go binding does not actually expose. Every API surface must be honest and tested.
Cross-platform is non-negotiable. The feature must work on macOS, Linux, and Windows. Windows ConPTY is a HIGH priority requirement and must be solved, not worked around.
No prepositions in method or function names (e.g. prefer LoadConfig over LoadFromConfig).

The termmux package MUST be designed for extraction into a standalone Go module. Zero imports from internal/builtin, internal/command, internal/scripting, or any osm-internal package. Only stdlib, creack/pty, golang.org/x/term, golang.org/x/sys, and its own sub-packages (pty, ptyio, vt, statusbar) are allowed.

ALL mutable state in the SessionManager MUST be owned by a single worker goroutine. Mutations flow through channels (request/reply ping-pong). Read-only access uses copy-on-write via atomic.Pointer. NO mutexes for session state. The ONLY acceptable mutex is EventBus.subscribers (subscriber list management).

Breaking changes are MANDATORY when the architecture requires them. No compatibility shims, no deprecated wrappers, no 'pragmatic' half-measures. Change the API, then update EVERY caller — Go code, JS shim, tests, scripts. The old code gets deleted.

mandatoryDirectives.contextManagement: Execute work in coherent vertical slices. Each task is cold-start-safe with explicit file targets, dependencies, and end-to-end acceptance. Keep the blueprint flat and sequential. Read scratch/termmux-architecture.md before any task in the 30-39 range.
mandatoryDirectives.implementationStandard: The goal is an exemplary, human-quality implementation with zero apparent AI slop. This means: code that reads like a human wrote it, tests that are exhaustive not approximate, error handling that is explicit not hand-waved, and end-to-end workflows that are proven to work, not speculated. Concurrency must be designed with the rigor of tmux's single-threaded event loop — translated to Go channels and goroutines, not mutexes.
mandatoryDirectives.reviewGate: Every task must pass two contiguous Rule of Two reviews (subagent verification) before marking Done. Code quality is verified through make make-all-with-log and project-specific integration tests. No task is complete without Rule of Two gate.
mandatoryDirectives.tmuxWisdom: tmux's architecture (single-threaded event loop, containment hierarchy, buffered event I/O, input routing via active session, delta rendering) is the reference model. The Go translation uses a single worker goroutine instead of an event loop, channels instead of callbacks, and atomic.Pointer instead of direct memory access. See scratch/termmux-architecture.md for full details.

statusSection.currentState: Blueprint expanded with comprehensive SessionManager architecture. Task 3 has been restructured as an architecture-first approach: Task 3 now captures the foundational constraints and defines the architecture document. Tasks 30-39 implement the architecture in self-contained, Jira-task-worthy units with full acceptance criteria. All downstream tasks (4+) depend on the SessionManager being complete. Architecture reference is scratch/termmux-architecture.md.
statusSection.goalState: Level 1 (Functional): User can run osm pr-split, interact with Claude pane, verification shell, and user shell interactively — inline and fullscreen passthrough — with seamless switching through a true multi-session manager. Windows ConPTY is fully supported. Level 2 (Quality): All interactions are responsive, rendering is pixel-perfect (no duplicate output, correct borders), focus system is reliable, error states are recoverable, code is exemplary (reads human-written), tests are exhaustive (no build tag violations). Level 3 (Validation): End-to-end tests prove workflows on all platforms; CI green on all checks; no performance regressions; no AI slop.
statusSection.definitionOfDone: The blueprint is Done when: (1) ALL tasks are marked Done, (2) each Done task has passed two contiguous Rule of Two reviews, (3) zero-uncovered-code-paths exist for the new functionality, (4) manual smoke tests confirm behavior across platforms, (5) docs are fully updated, and (6) all integration tests pass on all CI platforms.

replanLog.trigger: Rule of Two Pass 1 FAIL (attempt 1, session 2026-04-07) — 8 findings
replanLog.summary: Review findings: (F1-CRITICAL) Task 33↔34 dependency inversion — Task 33 used session.Reader() but Reader() was added by Task 34; fix: Task 34 now depends on [32], Task 33 depends on [32,34]. (F2-SIGNIFICANT) StatusBar silently dropped from Passthrough; fix: added StatusBar to PassthroughConfig in Task 36 and architecture doc. (F3-SIGNIFICANT) ScreenSnapshot missing FullScreen for terminal restoration; fix: added FullScreen field to ScreenSnapshot in Task 30, Task 33, and architecture doc (ANSI=SGR for TUI, FullScreen=CUP for restore). (F4-STRUCTURAL) Task 3 depended on Tasks 1+2 (ConPTY) needlessly delaying architecture; fix: Task 3 dependsOn=[]. (F5-MINOR) Task 37 migration map incomplete; fix: added SetClaudeStatus, ChildDone, PassthroughTarget/SetPassthroughTarget, callback→EventBus pattern, StringIO→StringIOSession adapter. (F6-MINOR) StringIO adapter missing for SessionManager.Register(); fix: added StringIOSession adapter to Task 37. (F7-MINOR) Task 38 Run() blocking would deadlock Goja; fix: explicit note that Go-side module spawns go mgr.Run(ctx) in background goroutine. Architecture doc also updated: shutdown ordering uses sorted session IDs (not random map iteration), StatusBar in PassthroughConfig, FullScreen in ScreenSnapshot, Run()/Goja deadlock note. Additional fixes from retry review: (H1) malformed JSON with duplicate content from truncated finalEnforcementProtocol entry — removed all duplicate content, properly closed the JSON; (H3) fixed ClaudeStatus getter reference (only setter exists); (H5) added reqActiveWriter, reqEnablePassthroughTee, reqDisablePassthroughTee to requestKind enum in both Task 30 and architecture doc. VTerm methods ContentANSI() and RenderFullScreen() verified to already exist.

replanLog.affectedTasks: Task 3 redefined; new tasks 30-39 inserted between tasks 3 and 4
replanLog.summary: Task 3 was a single monolithic task covering the entire SessionManager. This created execution risk: an agent starting Task 3 cold would improvise rather than follow a clear path. The task has been restructured: Task 3 retains the constraints and references the architecture document (scratch/termmux-architecture.md). Tasks 30-39 implement the architecture in self-contained units, each with specific file targets, clear dependencies, and verifiable acceptance criteria. Architectural decisions are documented in scratch/termmux-architecture.md based on deep analysis of the existing termmux codebase and the tmux C codebase (session.c, server.c, window.c, tmux.h). Key decisions: single worker goroutine owns all mutable state (inspired by tmux's single-threaded event loop), copy-on-write snapshots for read access (Go idiomatic alternative to tmux's direct memory), channel-based request/reply for mutations, EventBus for fan-out notifications, InteractiveSession interface trimmed to minimal contract (VTerm ownership moved to worker). All existing Mux callers will be migrated to SessionManager and the Mux type eliminated.

replanLog.trigger: Rule of Two Pass 1 Failure (attempt 4) - structural unknown violation
replanLog.summary: Inserted Replanning Checkpoint after Task 6 to respect 'Surface unknowns explicitly' constraint. Stripped explicit file expectations from downstream TUI tasks so they depend on the replan. Corrected Linux-specific prescriptiveness in process termination task.

replanLog.trigger: Rule of Two Pass 1 Failure (attempt 3) - autopsy-driven pivot
replanLog.summary: Rewrote blueprint to incorporate autopsy insights. Falsely completed tasks and critical gaps converted back to Not Started. Added replanLog. Fixed Task 9 logical paradox. Added Task 3 dependency to Task 5. Enforced testing.Short() in Task 11 E2E tests.

sequentialTasks.task: Add CaptureSession Interface Assertion (GAP-006)
sequentialTasks.description: Add a compile-time interface assertion `var _ InteractiveSession = (*CaptureSession)(nil)` to internal/termmux/capture.go to ensure it structurally implements InteractiveSession and prevent future silent API breakages.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: The build system structurally rejects any future refactors that break CaptureSession's interactive capabilities without corresponding API updates, ensuring developers immediately catch InteractiveSession contract violations at the compiler level rather than at runtime.
sequentialTasks.note: Already implemented at capture.go:628 — verified 2026-04-07. Both var _ io.Closer and var _ InteractiveSession assertions exist.

sequentialTasks.task: Implement ConPTY Windows support (GAP-005)
sequentialTasks.description: Windows PTY support is missing, currently dropping Windows to capture-only mode. Implement full ConPTY support in internal/termmux/pty/ and internal/termmux/platform_windows.go to bring interactive parity to Windows users.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: pr-split verify shell, Claude pane, and user shell are fully interactive on Windows via ConPTY, with seamless functional parity to macOS and Linux.

sequentialTasks.task: Define SessionManager architecture and constraints (GAP-001/007)
sequentialTasks.description: This task establishes the architectural foundation for all subsequent SessionManager work. Read and internalize scratch/termmux-architecture.md which defines: (1) Single worker goroutine owns all mutable state — no mutexes for session state — inspired by tmux's single-threaded event loop from server.c/proc.c; (2) Channel-based request/reply (ping-pong) for all mutations, modeled after tmux's command queue (cmdq); (3) Copy-on-write ScreenSnapshot via atomic.Pointer for non-blocking reads, replacing tmux's direct memory access with Go-idiomatic concurrent read patterns; (4) EventBus for fan-out event delivery (session registered/activated/output/exited/closed/resize/bell); (5) InteractiveSession interface trimmed to minimal contract (Write, Resize, Close, Done, Reader) — VTerm ownership moves from individual sessions to the managedSession wrapper in the worker; (6) Mux type eliminated entirely — SessionManager subsumes all Mux functionality; (7) Module-extractable design — zero imports from osm internal packages; (8) Containment hierarchy extensible to Windows/Layouts (tmux-like session→window→pane model for future). The architecture document (scratch/termmux-architecture.md) is the AUTHORITATIVE reference for tasks 30-39. No deviation without a replan log entry. Implementation must follow the phased approach: core types → worker → session integration → public API → Mux elimination → JS shim → caller migration.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: The architecture document exists at scratch/termmux-architecture.md, has been reviewed through Rule of Two, and serves as the single authoritative reference for the SessionManager implementation. All subsequent tasks (30-39) can be cold-started by reading this document first.
sequentialTasks.completedNote: scratch/termmux-architecture.md exists (~500 lines). Rule of Two passed in prior session (2 contiguous passes). Document covers: single worker goroutine, channel-based request/reply, COW snapshots, EventBus, InteractiveSession trimming, Mux elimination, module extractability.

sequentialTasks.task: Implement core SessionManager types and request/reply protocol
sequentialTasks.description: Create internal/termmux/manager.go and internal/termmux/manager_test.go. Define the foundational types: SessionID (uint64), SessionState enum (Created/Running/Exited/Closed with documented state transitions), ScreenSnapshot (immutable struct with Gen, PlainText, ANSI, FullScreen, Rows, Cols, Timestamp — where ANSI is SGR-attributed content for TUI embedding and FullScreen is CUP-positioned content for terminal restoration during passthrough re-entry), SessionInfo (immutable summary with ID, Target, State, IsActive). Define the SessionManager struct with fields: reqChan (chan request, cap 64), mergedOutput (chan sessionOutput, cap 64), eventBus (*EventBus), done (chan struct{}), sessions (map[SessionID]*managedSession — worker-owned), activeID (SessionID — worker-owned), nextID (SessionID — worker-owned), termRows/termCols (worker-owned). Define managedSession struct with fields: session (InteractiveSession), vterm (*vt.VTerm — worker-owned), snapshot (atomic.Pointer[ScreenSnapshot]), state (SessionState), target (SessionTarget), lastActive (time.Time), outputCh (<-chan []byte). Define the request/response protocol types: requestKind enum with values reqRegister, reqUnregister, reqActivate, reqInput, reqResize, reqSnapshot, reqActiveID, reqSessions, reqClose, reqActiveWriter, reqEnablePassthroughTee, reqDisablePassthroughTee (the last three support Passthrough mode per the architecture doc); request struct with kind/payload/reply channel; response struct with value/err. Define the sessionOutput struct (id SessionID, data []byte — nil means EOF). All types must have doc comments explaining their concurrency semantics. Tests: validate state transition logic (Created→Running→Exited→Closed, reject invalid transitions e.g. Closed→Running), validate ScreenSnapshot immutability (concurrent reads do not race with worker writes), validate SessionInfo construction from managedSession.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: A well-documented set of types in internal/termmux/manager.go forms the data foundation of the SessionManager. The types enforce the architectural invariants: SessionID is opaque and monotonic, SessionState transitions are validated, ScreenSnapshot is provably immutable (struct fields are value types, no pointer sharing — includes PlainText, ANSI, FullScreen), and the request/reply protocol is typed and safe. Unit tests in internal/termmux/manager_test.go prove state transition validation, snapshot immutability under concurrent access, and request/response round-trip correctness. `go vet`, `staticcheck`, and race detector pass. The types are usable by the next task (worker goroutine) without modification.
sequentialTasks.completedNote: manager.go (450 lines), eventbus.go (100 lines), manager_test.go (680 lines, 19 tests). Includes Run/Close/dispatch skeleton and JS shim wiring in module.go. Deadcode resolved by wiring NewSessionManager from osm:termmux JS module. Rule of Two passed (2 contiguous PASS reviews).

sequentialTasks.task: Implement EventBus with fan-out delivery and subscriber management
sequentialTasks.description: Create internal/termmux/eventbus.go and internal/termmux/eventbus_test.go. Implement EventBus providing typed, fan-out event delivery. Define EventKind enum (EventSessionRegistered, EventSessionActivated, EventSessionOutput, EventSessionExited, EventSessionClosed, EventResize, EventBell). Define Event struct (Kind, SessionID, Data any, Time). EventBus internals: mutex-protected subscriber map (map[int]chan<- Event), monotonic subscriber IDs, configurable per-subscriber buffer size (default 64). Methods: Subscribe(bufSize int) (id int, ch <-chan Event) — returns a read-only channel; Unsubscribe(id int); Publish(event Event) — iterates a SNAPSHOT of subscribers (copy the map under lock, release lock, then send), uses non-blocking sends to prevent slow subscribers from blocking the worker; Close() — closes all subscriber channels, clears map. The reason for the subscriber-list copy during Publish: the worker goroutine calls Publish, and we MUST NOT hold the mutex while sending to subscriber channels (which could block or be slow). The copy ensures the Publish critical section is O(subscribers) for the copy, then lock-free for delivery. Tests: verify fan-out (one publish reaches N subscribers), verify non-blocking (slow subscriber does not block Publish), verify Unsubscribe (removed subscriber gets no more events, channel is closed), verify Close (all channels closed, subsequent Publish is no-op), verify concurrent Subscribe/Unsubscribe/Publish under race detector.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: The EventBus in internal/termmux/eventbus.go provides reliable, non-blocking fan-out event delivery suitable for use by the SessionManager worker goroutine. Subscribers receive events on buffered channels without blocking the publisher. The implementation is the ONLY place in the termmux package that uses a sync.Mutex (protecting the subscriber map — an infrequent-mutation, concurrent-read data structure). All tests pass under `go test -race`. The EventBus is fully self-contained — no dependencies on SessionManager, making it independently testable and potentially useful outside termmux.

sequentialTasks.task: Implement worker goroutine with select loop and request dispatch
sequentialTasks.description: Add the Run() method and request dispatch to internal/termmux/manager.go. Extend tests in internal/termmux/manager_test.go. The worker goroutine (Run method) blocks on a select loop with three cases: (1) reqChan — dispatch to per-requestKind handlers; (2) mergedOutput — route session output to VTerm, publish snapshot, emit event; (3) ctx.Done() — graceful shutdown. Implement the request dispatch: handleRequest(req) switches on req.kind and calls the appropriate handler (handleRegister, handleUnregister, handleActivate, handleInput, handleResize, handleSnapshot, handleActiveID, handleSessions, handleClose). Each handler applies the mutation to worker-owned state, then sends the response on req.reply. Implement handleRegister: assigns nextID (post-increment), creates managedSession (state=Created, vterm=NewVTerm(termRows, termCols), wires outputCh), stores in sessions map, publishes EventSessionRegistered, replies with SessionID. Implement handleUnregister: validates ID exists, validates state != Closed, calls session.Close(), sets state=Closed, removes from map, if was activeID then activeID=0, publishes EventSessionClosed, replies with nil error. Implement handleActivate: validates ID exists and state is Running or Created, sets activeID, publishes EventSessionActivated, replies. Implement handleInput: validates activeID exists and state is Running, calls sessions[activeID].session.Write(data), replies with write result. Implement handleResize: updates termRows/termCols, for each session with state < Closed: resize VTerm, call session.Resize(), replies. Implement handleSnapshot: validates ID, reads managedSession.snapshot.Load(), replies with *ScreenSnapshot or nil. Implement handleActiveID: replies with activeID (zero value if none). Implement handleSessions: builds []SessionInfo from sessions map (value copies, not pointers), replies. Graceful shutdown: stop accepting requests, collect session IDs into sorted slice (descending — deterministic order, not random map iteration), close each session in sorted order, drain mergedOutput, close EventBus, close done channel. The Run method signature: Run(ctx context.Context) error. It blocks until ctx is canceled or Close is called. Tests: test Register→Activate→Input→Snapshot round trip with a mock InteractiveSession; test Unregister cleanup; test Activate invalid ID returns error; test graceful shutdown closes all sessions; test concurrent requests from multiple goroutines under race detector; test mergedOutput processing triggers VTerm update and snapshot publish.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: The SessionManager.Run() method starts a worker goroutine that processes all mutations via channel-based request dispatch. The worker goroutine is the SOLE owner of sessions map, activeID, nextID, termRows, termCols. No other goroutine reads or writes these fields. All request handlers are implemented and tested: Register returns a unique SessionID; Unregister cleans up; Activate switches the active session; Input routes to the active session; Resize broadcasts to all sessions; Snapshot returns the latest COW snapshot; ActiveID and Sessions return current state. Graceful shutdown is deterministic: all sessions are closed, resources are released, the done channel is closed. Tests prove the worker is safe under concurrent access (race detector clean) and that request/reply latency is bounded (worker does not block on I/O).

sequentialTasks.task: Implement merged output pipeline and session output processing
sequentialTasks.description: Implement the session output pipeline in internal/termmux/manager.go. PREREQUISITE: Task 34 must be complete — InteractiveSession now includes Reader() <-chan []byte. When a session is registered via handleRegister, the worker must start consuming the session's output. Each session's Reader() channel is read by a per-session goroutine that forwards to mergedOutput as sessionOutput{id, data}. In handleRegister, spawn a goroutine that reads from session.Reader(), wraps each chunk as sessionOutput{id, data}, and sends to mergedOutput. On EOF (channel closed), send sessionOutput{id, nil} as a sentinel, then return. In the worker select loop, handle mergedOutput: (1) if data is nil, mark session as Exited, publish EventSessionExited; (2) if data is non-nil, write to managedSession.vterm via vterm.Write(data), then create a new ScreenSnapshot{Gen: incrementing counter, PlainText: vterm.String(), ANSI: vterm.ContentANSI(), FullScreen: vterm.RenderFullScreen(), Rows: vterm.rows, Cols: vterm.cols, Timestamp: time.Now()}, store via managedSession.snapshot.Store(&snap), publish EventSessionOutput. Handle the BEL character: the existing VTerm has a BellFn callback. Since VTerm is worker-owned, set BellFn during session registration to publish EventBell via the EventBus. Since BellFn is called from VTerm.Write which is called from the worker goroutine, this is safe (no cross-goroutine callback). Tests: register a mock session that produces known output sequences → verify VTerm screen state matches expected content; verify ScreenSnapshot.PlainText, .ANSI, and .FullScreen are correct; verify snapshot Gen increments monotonically; verify EventSessionOutput events are published with correct SessionID; verify nil sentinel triggers state transition to Exited and EventSessionExited; verify BEL in output triggers EventBell. Use testing.Short() guard for tests that spawn real PTY processes.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: PTY output from any registered session flows through the merged output pipeline to the worker, which updates the session's VTerm screen buffer and publishes an immutable ScreenSnapshot (with FullScreen for terminal restoration) via atomic.Pointer. Any goroutine can read the latest snapshot at any time without blocking the worker. The output pipeline is provably non-blocking for the worker (VTerm.Write is CPU-bound, snapshot creation is fast, EventBus.Publish is non-blocking). Event subscribers receive EventSessionOutput and EventSessionExited notifications with correct SessionIDs and timing. The pipeline handles BEL characters via EventBell. Tests verify the entire data flow from mock session output to snapshot availability and event delivery.

sequentialTasks.task: Refactor InteractiveSession interface and migrate CaptureSession
sequentialTasks.description: This task makes the BREAKING CHANGE to InteractiveSession, then updates CaptureSession to conform. In internal/termmux/session.go, replace the current InteractiveSession interface with the trimmed version: Write([]byte) (int, error), Resize(rows, cols int) error, Close() error, Done() <-chan struct{}, Reader() <-chan []byte. Remove: Output(), Screen() (now provided by SessionManager snapshots), Target(), SetTarget() (now managed by SessionManager metadata), IsRunning() (now derived from SessionState). In internal/termmux/capture.go, remove the internal VTerm from CaptureSession (it's now worker-owned). CaptureSession.Start() must return a <-chan []byte that the SessionManager can consume (this replaces the internal readerLoop). The existing readerLoop goroutine is restructured: instead of draining to an internal VTerm, it drains to an exported channel. CaptureSession's readerLoop becomes: read from BufferedReader.Output(), forward each chunk to an output channel (returned by Reader()), send nil on EOF, call proc.Wait() for exit status, close done channel. Remove CaptureSession.Output(), CaptureSession.Screen(), CaptureSession.Target(), CaptureSession.SetTarget(), CaptureSession.IsRunning() — these are now SessionManager responsibilities. Update the compile-time interface assertion (from Task 1) to match the new interface. In internal/termmux/capture_test.go, update tests: remove tests for Output()/Screen() (these test VTerm integration which now lives in the SessionManager). Add tests for Reader() channel: verify output chunks arrive correctly, verify nil sentinel on process exit, verify Done() closes after Reader() drains. ALL existing tests in internal/termmux/ that reference the removed methods must be updated or removed. Run `make test` and `make lint` to verify nothing is broken.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: The InteractiveSession interface is trimmed to 5 methods (Write, Resize, Close, Done, Reader) — a minimal contract that any PTY-wrapping type can implement without coupling to VTerm or session metadata. CaptureSession conforms to the new interface: it no longer owns a VTerm, no longer tracks Target metadata, and exposes its PTY output via a Reader() channel. All removed functionality (screen capture, metadata) is provided by SessionManager as documented in scratch/termmux-architecture.md. All tests pass, the compile-time assertion is updated, and `make lint` is clean. The old Output()/Screen()/Target()/SetTarget()/IsRunning() methods are DELETED — no deprecated stubs.

sequentialTasks.task: Implement SessionManager public API methods with full test coverage
sequentialTasks.description: In internal/termmux/manager.go, implement the public API methods that callers use. Each method creates a request, sends it to reqChan, and blocks on the reply. Factory: NewSessionManager(...Option) *SessionManager — allocates channels, creates EventBus, does NOT start the worker (Run does that). Mutation methods: Register(session InteractiveSession, target SessionTarget) (SessionID, error); Unregister(id SessionID) error; Activate(id SessionID) error; Input(data []byte) error (routes to active session); Resize(rows, cols int) error (broadcasts). Query methods: Snapshot(id SessionID) *ScreenSnapshot (returns nil if not found); ActiveID() SessionID; Sessions() []SessionInfo (returns COW slice). Event methods: Subscribe(bufSize int) (int, <-chan Event); Unsubscribe(id int). Lifecycle: Run(ctx context.Context) error (blocks, starts worker); Close() error (signals worker to stop, blocks until done). Functional options: WithRows(int) Option, WithCols(int) Option, WithMergedOutputBuffer(int) Option, WithRequestBuffer(int) Option. Error behavior: methods called before Run() or after Close() return ErrManagerNotRunning. Handle reqChan full (all requests block — reqChan capacity should be generous, 64). In internal/termmux/manager_test.go, write comprehensive tests for every public method: TestNewSessionManager (verifies defaults), TestRegisterAndActivate (register two sessions, activate second, verify ActiveID changes), TestInput (routes to active session, verify mock received data), TestResize (verify all sessions resized), TestSnapshot (verify latest snapshot returned, verify nil for unknown ID), TestSessions (verify SessionInfo list matches registered sessions), TestSubscribe (verify events received), TestUnregister (verify session closed, verify snapshot returns nil), TestCloseGraceful (verify all sessions closed in order, verify Run returns), TestMethodsBeforeRun (verify ErrManagerNotRunning), TestConcurrentAccess (hammer all methods from 10 goroutines under race detector). Use skipSlow(t) or testing.Short() guard for any test spawning real processes.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: SessionManager provides a complete, typed, documented public API that callers (including the JS shim) use to manage terminal sessions. Every public method is tested with isolated, deterministic tests. The API enforces the architectural invariant: callers communicate with the worker exclusively via request/reply channels. COW snapshots are accessible at any time without blocking. Event subscription provides real-time notifications. The API is suitable for direct use by Go callers (e.g., internal/command/pr_split.go) and for wrapping by the JS shim (internal/builtin/termmux/module.go). All tests pass under `go test -race`.

sequentialTasks.task: Implement Passthrough mode in SessionManager
sequentialTasks.description: Add Passthrough(ctx context.Context, cfg PassthroughConfig) (ExitReason, error) to SessionManager in internal/termmux/manager.go. PassthroughConfig contains: Stdin (io.Reader), Stdout (io.Writer), TermFd (int), ToggleKey (byte), TermState (ptyio.TermState), BlockingGuard (ptyio.BlockingGuard), StatusBar (*statusbar.StatusBar, optional — when non-nil, a scroll region is set to reserve the bottom row, the StatusBar is rendered there, and the existing sgrmouse.filterMouseForStatusBar logic is applied to stdin to route mouse clicks on the status bar row). This matches the current Mux.RunPassthrough() UX which deeply integrates StatusBar (scroll regions, height subtraction, re-rendering after VTerm restore from ScreenSnapshot.FullScreen). Passthrough operates OUTSIDE the worker goroutine — it takes over stdin/stdout directly for low-latency I/O. The flow: (1) Validate activeID is set and session is Running via a request to the worker; (2) Enter raw terminal mode via TermState.MakeRaw(TermFd); (3) Ensure blocking via BlockingGuard.EnsureBlocking(TermFd); (4) Get the active session's PTY writer from the worker via a new request type (reqActiveWriter → returns io.Writer); (5) Spawn stdin→PTY goroutine: read from Stdin, scan for toggle key (reuse existing sgrmouse.filterMouseForStatusBar logic), write non-toggle bytes to PTY writer; (6) During passthrough, the worker continues receiving output via mergedOutput and updating snapshots — this is the key difference from the old Mux.RunPassthrough where the teeLoop handled forwarding; (7) Additionally, subscribe to EventSessionOutput for the active session and forward the raw output chunks (pre-VTerm) to Stdout — OR wire a second output path: the per-session reader goroutine tees raw chunks to both mergedOutput AND a passthrough-specific channel; (8) Wait for: toggle key → ExitToggle, session Done → ExitChildExit, ctx.Done → ExitContext, error → ExitError; (9) Restore terminal state; (10) Return exit reason. For the stdout forwarding during passthrough, the cleanest approach is: when passthrough starts, send a request to the worker to enable a raw output tee for the active session. The worker sets a passthroughWriter on the managedSession. The output processing in the worker goroutine writes raw chunks to both VTerm AND the passthroughWriter. When passthrough ends, send a request to disable the tee. The passthroughWriter is an atomic.Pointer[io.Writer] on managedSession — the worker checks it on each output chunk. Tests: TestPassthroughToggleKey (verify toggle key press exits with ExitToggle), TestPassthroughChildExit (verify child exit returns ExitChildExit), TestPassthroughContext (verify context cancel returns ExitContext), TestPassthroughInputForwarding (verify stdin bytes reach PTY), TestPassthroughOutputForwarding (verify PTY output reaches stdout), TestPassthroughTerminalRestore (verify raw mode is restored on all exit paths, including panic). Use testing.Short() guard.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Users can enter passthrough mode for any active session via SessionManager.Passthrough(). The active session's PTY gets direct stdin forwarding with toggle key detection (matching existing UX). When StatusBar is configured, a scroll region reserves the bottom row for status bar rendering, mouse events on the status bar row are routed correctly, and terminal restoration uses ScreenSnapshot.FullScreen for flicker-free re-entry — matching the existing Mux.RunPassthrough() UX exactly. PTY output is both forwarded to stdout in real-time (low latency) and processed by the worker for VTerm capture (best of both worlds). Terminal state (raw mode, blocking flags) is always restored on exit, including error paths. The passthrough implementation does NOT introduce additional mutexes — it communicates with the worker via requests and uses atomic.Pointer for the tee writer. All exit reasons work correctly. Tests prove correct behavior for toggle key, child exit, context cancel, and terminal restoration.

sequentialTasks.task: Eliminate Mux type — migrate all callers to SessionManager
sequentialTasks.description: Delete internal/termmux/termmux.go (the Mux type) and internal/termmux/termmux_test.go. This is the culminating breaking change. The SessionManager now provides all functionality that Mux provided. Migration map for every Mux method: Mux.New() → NewSessionManager(); Mux.Attach(session) → mgr.Register(session, target) + mgr.Activate(id); Mux.Detach() → mgr.Unregister(id); Mux.RunPassthrough() → mgr.Passthrough(ctx, cfg); Mux.ChildScreen() → mgr.Snapshot(id).ANSI; Mux.ChildExitOutput() → mgr.Snapshot(id).PlainText; Mux.WriteToChild(data) → mgr.Input(data); Mux.HasChild() → mgr.ActiveID() != 0; Mux.ActiveTarget() → info from mgr.Sessions(); Mux.SetActiveTarget() → tracked by SessionManager metadata; Mux.ActiveSide() → concept replaced by mgr.ActiveID(); Mux.SetBellFunc() → mgr.Subscribe() for EventBell; Mux.SetOutputFunc() → mgr.Subscribe() for EventSessionOutput; Mux.SetToggleKey() → PassthroughConfig.ToggleKey; Mux.SetStatusEnabled() → config option; Mux.SetResizeFunc() → mgr.Subscribe() for EventResize + custom handler; Mux.Session() → mgr.Snapshot(id); Mux.LastWriteTime() → ScreenSnapshot.Timestamp; Mux.SetClaudeStatus() → SessionTarget metadata or StatusBar direct call (no getter exists on Mux - only a setter that delegates to StatusBar.SetClaudeStatus); Mux.ChildDone() → mgr.Subscribe() for EventSessionExited or session.Done() channel; Mux.PassthroughTarget()/SetPassthroughTarget() → PassthroughConfig.Target or tracked by SessionManager via ActiveID(); callback patterns (SetBellFunc, SetOutputFunc, SetResizeFunc) → EventBus subscriptions (each callback becomes a subscriber goroutine that receives events and executes the callback logic). Also migrate StringIO/WrapStringIO: currently produces io.ReadWriteCloser for Mux.Attach(). Create a StringIOSession adapter type that wraps StringIO and implements the new InteractiveSession interface (5 methods: Write, Resize, Close, Done, Reader) — Reader() returns a channel fed by StringIO's read side, Done() returns a channel closed on Close(), Resize is no-op. Update WrapStringIO in the JS shim to use StringIOSession. Update internal/command/pr_split.go: replace `termmux.New(os.Stdin, stdout, termFd)` with `termmux.NewSessionManager(termmux.WithRows(rows), termmux.WithCols(cols))`, start worker with `go mgr.Run(ctx)`, register sessions via `mgr.Register()`. Delete the MuxSession type (internal/termmux/termmux.go) — its functionality is fully replaced. Update all mux-related integration tests. Run `make test`, `make lint`, `deadcode` to verify nothing is broken and no dead code remains. Also update internal/termmux/doc.go to reflect the new architecture.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: The Mux type is DELETED. termmux.go no longer exists. Every call site that used Mux now uses SessionManager with the equivalent API. The SessionManager is the sole entry point for terminal multiplexing. No compatibility shims or deprecated wrappers exist — the old code is gone. All tests pass, `make lint` is clean, `deadcode` reports no regressions (Mux methods that were wired are now replaced by SessionManager methods that are wired). The doc.go package comment accurately describes the new architecture.

sequentialTasks.task: Rewrite JS shim (osm:termmux module) for SessionManager API
sequentialTasks.description: Rewrite internal/builtin/termmux/module.go to wrap SessionManager instead of Mux. The current module exports newMux() which wraps a pre-created Mux. The new module exports newSessionManager() which creates and wraps a SessionManager. JS API changes: (1) termmux.newSessionManager({rows, cols}) → creates SessionManager. CRITICAL: the JS constructor must NOT call Run() directly (it blocks and would deadlock the single-threaded Goja runtime). Instead, the Go-side module initialization spawns `go mgr.Run(ctx)` in a background goroutine with a context derived from the JS runtime's lifecycle. The JS constructor returns after Run() is started asynchronously; (2) mgr.register(session, {name, kind, id}) → returns sessionID (number); (3) mgr.unregister(id) → removes session; (4) mgr.activate(id) → switches active session; (5) mgr.input(data) → routes to active; (6) mgr.resize(rows, cols) → broadcasts; (7) mgr.snapshot(id) → returns {gen, plainText, ansi, rows, cols, timestamp}; (8) mgr.activeID() → returns number; (9) mgr.sessions() → returns [{id, target: {name, kind, id}, state, isActive}]; (10) mgr.passthrough({stdin, stdout, termFd, toggleKey, ...}) → blocking, returns {reason, error?}; (11) mgr.on(event, callback) → subscribe to EventBus events (convert Go Event to JS objects); (12) mgr.off(id) → unsubscribe; (13) mgr.pollEvents() → drain async events (use existing muxEvents pattern adapted for EventBus); (14) mgr.close() → graceful shutdown. Remove old Mux wrapper code (WrapMux, old switchTo, old fromModel). Remove the WrapMux function entirely. The event delivery pattern: spawn a goroutine that subscribes to the SessionManager's EventBus and forwards events to the muxEvents pending channel (existing async queue). pollEvents() continues to work as before — drain pending, emit to JS callbacks. For the CaptureSession JS wrapper: keep wrapCaptureSession() but update it to match the new InteractiveSession interface (no more output(), screen(), target(), setTarget() — these are now on the SessionManager wrapper). Keep the newCaptureSession() factory. Update internal/builtin/register.go if the Require function signature changed. Tests: update all JS-level tests that reference termmux APIs. Run `make test-prsplit-fast` and `make test-prsplit-all`.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: The JS shim (osm:termmux module) exposes the full SessionManager API to JavaScript. JS code can create a SessionManager, register multiple sessions, switch between them, read COW snapshots, send input, enter passthrough mode, and subscribe to events. The JS API is a 1:1 mapping of the Go public API — no hidden functionality, no unsupported methods. All JS-level tests pass. The old WrapMux code is deleted. Event delivery uses the EventBus subscription → async queue → pollEvents() → JS callback pipeline. pr_split JS scripts can be updated to use the new API (next task).

sequentialTasks.task: Migrate pr_split JS scripts from Mux API to SessionManager API
sequentialTasks.description: Update all pr_split JavaScript files to use the new SessionManager-based termmux API. The key change: pr_split.go no longer creates a Mux and passes it as `tuiMux` global. Instead, it creates a SessionManager and passes it as the `sessionMgr` global (or an appropriate name — inspect the current global setup in internal/command/pr_split.go around lines 474-495). Migrate every JS file that uses `tuiMux.*`: (1) pr_split_16f_tui_model.js — replace tuiMux.attach/switchTo/childScreen/writeToChild with sessionMgr.register/activate/passthrough/snapshot/input calls; (2) pr_split_16d_tui_handlers_claude.js — replace Claude session management with sessionMgr.register for Claude session, sessionMgr.activate; (3) pr_split_16e_tui_update.js — replace screen reads with sessionMgr.snapshot(id).ansi; (4) All other pr_split_16*.js files that reference tuiMux — search with grep for 'tuiMux' across internal/command/. Update internal/command/pr_split.go: replace `termmux.New()` with `termmux.NewSessionManager()`, replace `termmuxmod.WrapMux()` with the new module wrapper, update the global name. Delete any remaining references to the old Mux API. Run the full test suite: `make test-prsplit-fast`, `make test-prsplit-all`, `make integration-test-prsplit-mcp`. Verify no JS runtime errors by running pr_split in a test environment.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: All pr_split JavaScript files use the SessionManager API exclusively. There are zero references to the old Mux API (tuiMux.attach, tuiMux.switchTo, etc.) anywhere in the codebase. The pr_split workflow functions identically to before: Claude pane works, verification shell works, user shell works, passthrough works, session switching works. All pr_split tests pass. The migration is invisible to the end user — same UX, fundamentally better architecture.

sequentialTasks.task: Implement fullscreen passthrough for any interactive session (GAP-008)
sequentialTasks.description: Currently, only Claude gets true passthrough. Implement true fullscreen passthrough functionality for the Verify Pane and user shell via CaptureSession. The SessionManager.Passthrough() method already supports any active session — the work here is in the TUI layer: update the JS code to allow Ctrl+] from any pane (not just Claude) to enter passthrough for that pane's session. Modify the TUI keymap handler to: (1) Detect which SessionID is currently focused in the TUI; (2) Call sessionMgr.activate(focusedID) + sessionMgr.passthrough(cfg) for that session. Update the TUI input routing in the decomposed handlers (post-Task 6) to handle the keystrokes correctly. This depends on Task 39 (JS migration) being complete so the correct API is available.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Users can hit Ctrl+] from the Verify Pane or user shell and get a fullscreen passthrough experience (not just inline). Session switching works flawlessly while in passthrough. The passthrough activates for whichever pane has focus, not hardcoded to Claude.

sequentialTasks.task: Support 'Ask Claude' in pr-split regardless of auto mode (GAP-002)
sequentialTasks.description: The 'Ask Claude' button in pr-split throws an error in non-auto modes. Update the TUI handlers so that the 'Ask Claude' workflow spawns a Claude session on-demand via sessionMgr.register() and works whenever the button is displayed, regardless of the user's strategy mode. The SessionManager makes this cleaner: registering a new session is explicit and doesn't conflict with existing sessions.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Clicking 'Ask Claude' successfully opens the Claude session and allows the user to get help in all pr-split modes (auto, manual, heuristic, directory). No auto-mode gating for this feature.

sequentialTasks.task: Decompose wizardUpdateImpl god function (KILL-004)
sequentialTasks.description: Decompose the ~600-line wizardUpdateImpl god function in pr_split_16e_tui_update.js into per-message-type handler dispatch functions. Separate key messages, mouse messages, window resizes, tick messages, and overlays into independently testable components.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: wizardUpdateImpl is reduced to a concise, top-level dispatch function. The monolithic structure is refactored into modular handlers with zero behavioral regressions.

sequentialTasks.task: Checkpoint: Replan TUI routing tasks based on new modular structure
sequentialTasks.description: Research Task & Replanning Checkpoint: Inspect the output of Task 6 to map the new modular handler structure. Do not guess the new file names or function structures. Locate where verify messages, key captures, and pane switching now live in the refactored TUI. Re-write all subsequent TUI-dependent tasks (like Verify session leak fix and TUI keymap docs) with exact, concrete file pointers based on this new reality. Proceed to next task ONLY after the blueprint has been replanned.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: The planner has explored the post-Task-6 codebase and updated the blueprint. Dependent tasks are heavily revised to point to the actual, correct un-papered file and function targets. A Replan Log entry is recorded.

sequentialTasks.task: Fix border formatting around autodetect/main values for source and target branch on PR split wizard configure page (GAP-003)
sequentialTasks.description: The borders surrounding the autodetect and main/default values for the source branch and target branch fields on the PR split wizard's configure page are broken and clip/wrap based on terminal constraints. Write a failing test that captures the broken rendering at multiple terminal sizes. Then fix the border rendering code in pr_split_15c_tui_screens.js so the test passes. Ensure borders render correctly at any terminal size without clipping or wrapping incorrectly.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Configure page renders cleanly with correctly-aligned borders around source and target branch fields at every terminal size. Golden-file tests exist proving the fix works across sizes.
sequentialTasks.completedNote: Two bugs fixed in pr_split_15c_tui_screens.js: (1) Width constant changed from -8 to -6 (correct accounting: indent=2+spacer=2+border=2=6; lipgloss Width() is content+padding, borders are added on top). (2) Multi-line indent changed from '  '+joinHorizontal(...) (only indents line 1) to lipgloss.newStyle().paddingLeft(2).render(joinHorizontal(...)) which indents all 5 lines of the bordered card block. Failing test TestChunk13_ViewConfigScreen_BorderVerticalAlignment added to pr_split_13_tui_test.go — tests border alignment at widths 60/80/120 by stripping ANSI and comparing rune positions of border characters across all lines in each card block. Test verified failing before fix (24 errors: col 17 vs 15) and passing after fix.

sequentialTasks.task: Fix Verify session leak on branch re-order (KILL-001)
sequentialTasks.description: DEPENDS ON REPLAN (Task 7) AND SessionManager migration (Task 39): With the SessionManager, fixing the verify session leak becomes explicit: when the user re-orders branches, call sessionMgr.unregister(oldVerifyID) to close the old session, then sessionMgr.register(newVerifySession, target) to start the new one. The Unregister method handles Close() → SIGTERM → SIGKILL → resource cleanup. Locate the exact handler in the decomposed TUI (post-Task 7 replan) that handles branch re-ordering, and wire the unregister+register sequence. The SessionManager's deterministic lifecycle (Created→Running→Exited→Closed) guarantees no leaks.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Moving between branch verification states cleanly shuts down the previous verify session and starts the new one without output leakage or hanging orphaned processes. The SessionManager's lifecycle tracking makes this behavior verifiable via the Sessions() query — old session is gone from the list.

sequentialTasks.task: Fix Claude orphan on double-SIGINT (KILL-002)
sequentialTasks.description: When a user hits double-SIGINT in internal/command/pr_split.go, os.Exit(130) runs without executing defers, skipping Claude executor cleanly and leaving Claude worker process groups orphaned. The fix: intercept the double-SIGINT and call sessionMgr.Close() synchronously before os.Exit(130). SessionManager.Close() performs deterministic shutdown: cancels all sessions, waits for Done() channels, SIGTERM→SIGKILL sequence. This ensures all PTY child processes are cleaned up. For the case where the SessionManager's shutdown hangs (process won't die), add a hard timeout (e.g., 5 seconds) after which os.Exit(130) proceeds anyway. Use platform-appropriate primitives: process groups on Unix (syscall.Kill(-pid, sig)), Job Objects on Windows.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Double Ctrl+C exits the program immediately but strictly guarantees all attached session processes are killed via SessionManager.Close() across Linux, macOS, and Windows without orphaned background processes remaining. A hard timeout ensures the UI doesn't hang indefinitely on a zombie process.

sequentialTasks.task: Remove -tags=integration build tag violation (GAP-009)
sequentialTasks.description: Remove the usage of `-tags=integration` and `+build integration` tags from the codebase. Rely exclusively on `testing.Short()` / `-short` flag for skipping slow execution, according to project rules, and update project.mk accordingly.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Developers and CI systems can reliably partition fast vs. slow tests using standard Go test flags (`go test -short`) without needing custom build tags. Standard Go tooling (like gopls and IDE test runners) works flawlessly out of the box without requiring special build constraint configuration.
sequentialTasks.completedNote: Removed all //go:build integration tags and -tags=integration from build commands. Replaced build-tag pair (goprompt_integration.go + goprompt_notintegration.go) with single runtime env-var-based file (goprompt_sync.go) using OSM_SYNC_PROTOCOL=1. Updated 9 termmux integration tests and 3 ptyio integration tests with testing.Short() skip guards. Updated project.mk integration-test-termmux target. Added OSM_SYNC_PROTOCOL=1 to all termtest WithEnv calls and TestMain environments across 6 test files. Updated platform-support-strategy.md docs.

sequentialTasks.task: Implement E2E Claude integration test with a mock binary (GAP-010)
sequentialTasks.description: Current pipeline tests rely heavily on mock MCP servers rather than a true binary. Implement an end-to-end integration test in internal/command/pr_split_binary_e2e_test.go that uses a generic mock binary specifically representing the real Claude CLI to verify process management, full lifecycle connection, and termination via SessionManager. You MUST include a testing.Short() or skipSlow(t) guard at the top of the test function because it spawns subprocesses, strictly adhering to AGENTS.md test rules.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: An E2E test runs the overarching pr-split workflow by spawning a mock Claude binary locally, proving that process management works seamlessly with the SessionManager and PTYs end-to-end. The test safely skips when 'go test -short' is run, ensuring fast feedback loops are not polluted.

sequentialTasks.task: Concrete UX refinement and TUI keymap docs
sequentialTasks.description: DEPENDS ON REPLAN (Task 7): First, execute the replan to locate where TUI keys are registered and mapped in the post-Task-6 structure. Then make the TUI shortcuts mechanically verifiable: Implement Ctrl+Tab to navigate panes without eating input. Tie the '?' key to open a Lipgloss-rendered modal with all registered shortcuts by capturing the key and rendering it in internal/command/pr_split_15d_tui_dialogs.js. Add a markdown table to docs/reference/tui-api.md matching 1:1 with TUI keymap registers.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: Ctrl+Tab navigates panes without eating input; ? opens a Lipgloss-rendered modal with all registered shortcuts; Docs contain a markdown table matching 1:1 with TUI keymap registers.

sequentialTasks.task: Benchmark pane switching and render pipeline
sequentialTasks.description: Add a performance test using testing.B in internal/command/pr_split_16_bench_test.go that benchmarks the pane switching and rendering latency via SessionManager. Validate that switching between the Claude session and user shell takes < 50ms per switch. Use pprof if needed to isolate allocation bottlenecks in the render path.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: A benchmark test `BenchmarkPaneSwitching` runs locally. Pprof output shows pane switch latency consistently below 50ms at scale with 1000 simulated log lines.

sequentialTasks.task: Add fuzz testing to SessionManager routing logic
sequentialTasks.description: Add a go fuzz test `FuzzSessionRouter` in internal/termmux/manager_test.go to bombard the SessionManager with parallel, random byte sequences and session-switch commands (Register, Activate, Input, Unregister in random order) to find race conditions, panics, or deadlocks in the worker goroutine.
sequentialTasks.acceptance: The SessionManager worker goroutine is proved practically to survive chaotic, interleaved requests and I/O from concurrent sessions, guaranteeing that no sequence of rapid session switching by the user while background streams are active will ever cause a deadlock, panic, or freeze the CLI. The fuzz test runs under `go test -fuzz` and discovers no issues after at least 30 seconds of fuzzing.

continuousVerification.coverageRequirement: 100% unit test coverage for SessionManager, EventBus, and all request handlers. 100% coverage for InteractiveSession interface implementations. Integration tests must exercise end-to-end workflows including session registration, activation, output pipeline, passthrough, and graceful shutdown on all supported platforms.

finalEnforcementProtocol: Every task marked Done must pass two contiguous Rule of Two reviews (subagent verification) before the mark is final.
All project-defined checks must pass on all project-defined platforms before a task is considered complete.
Acceptance criteria are the source of truth for 'Done', not just code existence or tests passing.
Do not accept 'good enough' — the goal is exemplary, human-quality code with zero apparent AI slop.
If stuck or uncertain, re-read scratch/termmux-architecture.md and this blueprint's Definition of Done section.

The termmux package MUST have zero imports from osm-internal packages. Verify with: grep -rn 'internal/' internal/termmux/*.go | grep -v '_test.go' — the output must be empty (only sub-package imports like termmux/pty, termmux/vt etc. are allowed).

ALL mutable state mutations in SessionManager MUST go through the worker goroutine's request channel. If you find yourself adding a mutex to SessionManager, STOP — you are violating the architecture. Re-read scratch/termmux-architecture.md.
Breaking changes are the expectation, not the exception. When the architecture requires changing an interface, change it and update ALL callers. No shims, no wrappers, no 'we'll migrate later'.
